**Gould's wattled bat** (***Chalinolobus gouldii***) is a species of [Australian](/wiki/Australia "Australia") [wattled bat](/wiki/Chalinolobus "Chalinolobus") named after the [English](/wiki/England "England") [naturalist](/wiki/Naturalist "Naturalist") [John Gould](/wiki/John_Gould "John Gould").[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gould%27s_wattled_bat&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

*C. gouldii* is the largest of the genus *Chalinolobus*, a group distinguished by fleshy lobes located at the corners of its mouth. For individuals throughout their range, the average length (head and body) is 70 ± 5 mm and average mass is 14 ± 4 [g](/wiki/Gram "Gram").[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3) The fur on the back is dark brown, becoming black on the head and shoulders.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Gould%27s_Wattled_Bat_in_care.jpg/250px-Gould%27s_Wattled_Bat_in_care.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gould%27s_Wattled_Bat_in_care.jpg)

## Distribution and habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gould%27s_wattled_bat&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Distribution and habitat")]

*C. gouldii* is known throughout mainland Australia (excluding northern [Cape York Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula") and [Nullarbor Plain](/wiki/Nullarbor_Plain "Nullarbor Plain")) as well as [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia"), and [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island").[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)

*C. gouldii* is found in a variety of habitats. In wooded areas, they are mostly arboreal, though they have also been found in the stumps and hollow limbs of trees or in bird nests. They may also inhabit urban settings, such as the ceilings and basements of buildings. While some individuals (usually males) may roost alone, colonies often hold about 30 bats. Colonies of up to 200 individuals are also known.[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)

## Behaviour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gould%27s_wattled_bat&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Behaviour")]

*C. gouldii* is active year-round across most of its range, but enters [hibernation](/wiki/Hibernation "Hibernation") in cooler climates. This torpor typically takes place throughout winter (May to early September) but has been observed as late as December.[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)

*C. gouldii* is the most common species found roosting in bat boxes installed at the [Organ Pipes National Park](/wiki/Organ_Pipes_National_Park "Organ Pipes National Park") in Melbourne, Victoria, accounting for 97% of species found. They are harp-trapped within the [Organ Pipes National Park](/wiki/Organ_Pipes_National_Park "Organ Pipes National Park") but the proportions vary from year to year. They also account for 95% of bats found in roost boxes in Wilson Reserve, Ivanhoe East, Melbourne, and 100% of bats found 1.5 km further east in Kew[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)

## Diet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gould%27s_wattled_bat&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Diet")]

*C. gouldii* is insectivorous; in much of its range, [moths](/wiki/Moth "Moth") are the most common food item, though [beetles](/wiki/Beetle "Beetle") play this role in the [riparian](/wiki/Riparian_zone "Riparian zone") woodlands of Tasmania. Other known prey includes [cockroaches](/wiki/Cockroach "Cockroach"), [flies](/wiki/Fly "Fly"), [stoneflies](/wiki/Plecoptera "Plecoptera"), [orthopterans](/wiki/Orthoptera "Orthoptera"), [hemipterans](/wiki/Hemiptera "Hemiptera"), [hymenopterans](/wiki/Hymenoptera "Hymenoptera") and other [lepidopterans](/wiki/Lepidopteran "Lepidopteran"), including [caterpillars](/wiki/Caterpillar "Caterpillar"). Grass seeds and twig fragments are occasionally ingested as well.[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)

## Reproduction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gould%27s_wattled_bat&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reproduction")]

The reproductive behaviour of *C. gouldii*, including the months of gestation, vary among different regions. In [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"), pregnancy usually occurs during September and October, lactation during November and December, and fledged young during December and January. In Western Australia, the birthing period (6–8 weeks) begins from late September to November depending on the latitude of the population involved.

Females can store fertile sperm for at least 33 days, allowing them to conceive long after mating. Because pregnancy occurs in both [uterine horns](/wiki/Uterine_horns "Uterine horns"), the resulting offspring are often twins.[[3]](#cite_note-Mammalian-3)
